## Video 1: I219 — "The Blood Test Your Doctor Forgets After 60"

**Working Title:** The Blood Test Your Doctor Forgets After 60 — And What It Predicts
**Pillar:** Diagnostic Gap / Cardiovascular / Advocacy

**Recommended:** Variant A — uses the "diagnostic gap" frame (P020/P030 pattern) that Dr. Kane owns. "Your Doctor Forgets" implies system failure without being anti-doctor. The "And What It Predicts" suffix adds forward-looking urgency.

**Backup:** Variant B — uses the "misdirection reveal" frame (P008) that performs at 17+ score. Good for A/B testing if Variant A CTR <5% at 24h.

### SEO Description

**First 2 sentences (critical — shown in search + above fold):**
> There's a genetic blood test that predicts your heart attack risk — and most doctors never order it. It's called Lipoprotein(a), or Lp(a), and 20% of the population has a dangerously elevated level without knowing it.

**Full description:**
```
There's a genetic blood test that predicts your heart attack risk — and most doctors never order it. It's called Lipoprotein(a), or Lp(a), and 20% of the population has a dangerously elevated level without knowing it.

In this video, Dr. Victor Kane explains why standard cholesterol panels miss this critical risk factor, who should get tested, and exactly what to say to your doctor to request it.

⏱️ TIMESTAMPS:
0:00 — The heart attack that shouldn't have happened
1:15 — What is Lp(a) and why genetics matter
3:42 — Why your standard lipid panel misses it
5:18 — The exact words to say to your doctor
7:05 — What your Lp(a) number means for your future
8:30 — What to do if your Lp(a) is elevated

📌 KEY TAKEAWAYS:
• Lp(a) is genetically determined — diet and exercise don't change it
• It's a one-time test (levels are stable for life)
• Normal LDL does NOT mean low cardiovascular risk if Lp(a) is elevated
• The test costs $30-50 and is covered by most insurance
• Elevated Lp(a) = cardiovascular risk equivalent to LDL of 190

⚠️ IMPORTANT: This video is for educational purposes. Always discuss testing and treatment decisions with your doctor. Dr. Kane does not recommend stopping or changing any medication without medical supervision.

📚 REFERENCES:
• Nordestgaard BG et al., European Heart Journal, 2010
• Tsimikas S., Journal of the American College of Cardiology, 2019
• Lp(a) Foundation clinical guidelines

🔔 SUBSCRIBE for weekly videos on vascular health, diagnostic gaps, and body signals your doctor might miss.

```

**Working Title:** The Morning Pattern in Your Legs After 60 That Predicts Heart Failure
**Pillar:** Cardiovascular / Body Signal / Predictive

### Title Variations (A/B/C)

| Variant | Title | Chars |
|---------|-------|-------|

| **B** | Why Your Legs Are Heavy Every Morning After 60 — It's Not Your Veins | 66 |
| **C** | Morning Legs Heavy After 60 But Fine at Night? Your Heart Is Warning You | 70 |

**Recommended:** Variant A — uses the "temporal pattern recognition" frame that is unique to this idea. "Predicts Heart Failure" is the strongest emotional trigger in the title while remaining accurate (HFpEF is the most underdiagnosed form of heart failure). The specificity of "Morning Pattern" creates curiosity gap.

**Backup:** Variant C — uses the paradox observation frame (P066/P098 pattern, score 18-19). The question format + "Your Heart Is Warning You" creates urgency. Slightly over 70 chars — trim to "Morning Legs Heavy After 60 But Fine at Night? Heart Warning" (64 chars) if needed.

### SEO Description

**First 2 sentences (critical — shown in search + above fold):**
> If your legs feel heavy and stiff every morning for the first hour — but feel fine by evening — it could be an early sign of heart failure that standard tests miss. This pattern is the opposite of what most seniors expect, and it's the key to catching HFpEF before it progresses.

**Full description:**
```
If your legs feel heavy and stiff every morning for the first hour — but feel fine by evening — it could be an early sign of heart failure that standard tests miss. This pattern is the opposite of what most seniors expect, and it's the key to catching HFpEF before it progresses.

In this video, Dr. Victor Kane explains why morning leg heaviness that IMPROVES during the day is a specific indicator of heart failure with preserved ejection fraction — the most common and most underdiagnosed form of heart failure in seniors.

⏱️ TIMESTAMPS:
0:00 — The pattern nobody explains
1:30 — Why morning legs feel heavy (the fluid redistribution problem)
3:45 — HFpEF: The "invisible heart failure" your echo might miss
5:20 — Morning vs. evening: The diagnostic pattern doctors overlook
7:00 — What to ask your doctor (exact words)
8:15 — 3 things you can do starting today

📌 KEY TAKEAWAYS:
• Morning leg heaviness that improves by evening = possible early HFpEF
• This is the OPPOSITE of venous insufficiency (which worsens through the day)
• Standard echo measures ejection fraction — but HFpEF has "normal" ejection fraction
• Ask your doctor: "Can you assess my diastolic function on echo?"
• HFpEF accounts for 50% of heart failure cases in seniors

⚠️ IMPORTANT: This video is for educational purposes. Leg heaviness can have many causes. Always discuss symptoms with your doctor for proper evaluation. Dr. Kane does not diagnose or treat any condition.

📚 REFERENCES:
• Paulus WJ & Tschöpe C., Journal of the American College of Cardiology, 2013
• Shah SJ et al., Circulation, 2023 (HFpEF guidelines)
• European Heart Journal, 2023 (WHtR mortality data)

🔔 SUBSCRIBE for weekly videos on vascular health, body signals, and diagnostic gaps after 60.

```
